title: What curtilage actually is
paras: Let's start with what curtilage actually is. There's no definition of curtilage in the Town and Country Planning Act 1990 — the concept is built entirely from case law. Dyer against Dorset County Council, reported at 1989, 1 Q-B 346, describes land forming one enclosure with the dwellinghouse. Part of the same physical and functional unit — not necessarily walled or fenced. Sutcliffe against Calderdale Borough Council, reported at 1982, 46 P and C-R 399, supplies the three working factors. Physical layout — the relationship between house and land. Ownership, past and present. And use or function — whether the land is used in connection with the house. None of these is individually decisive; together they build a picture. And Skerritts of Nottingham, reported at 2001, Q-B 59, disposed of the idea that curtilage must be small. What matters is relationship, not size. A half-acre walled garden can be wholly within curtilage, while a detached paddock across a track is not.

Start with what curtilage is not. It's not the red line on a site plan, not the extent of ownership, and not whatever happens to be mown. You can own ten acres around a house without having ten acres of curtilage. Nor is domestic use conclusive. Burford against the Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government, reported at 2017, E-W-H-C 1493, in the Administrative Court, is the sharpest illustration. A certificate had confirmed the land was lawfully used for purposes incidental to the dwelling — barbecues, football, recreational horses. Yet when a building went up on it under claimed Class E rights, the land was held to be outside the curtilage. It was fenced and hedged off from the house, formerly paddock — not one enclosure with it. The CLEUD confirmed incidental use; it did not, and could not, confirm curtilage. The two tests are related, but they're distinct.

Its planning role is specific and unforgiving. Curtilage defines the land within which Class E permitted development rights operate — the phrase 'within the curtilage' is a gatekeeper, and a building outside it needs express permission, however small and domestic. It decides enforcement cases the same way. And in listed building law — section 1, subsection 5, paragraph b of the Listed Buildings Act 1990 — it determines what is swept into the listing. A stable or garage within the curtilage, and present at the time of listing, is protected though never named. And the threshold there is arguably tighter still. Methuen-Campbell against Walters, reported at 1979, Q-B 525, requires an intimate association with the listed building — not mere proximity, not recent connection.

The assessment is fact and degree — planning-speak for judgment, exercised site by site. Physical relationship: is the land directly adjacent, accessible without crossing other land, enclosed with the house — whether formally, or by clear visual boundaries? Function: is it part of day-to-day domestic life — drying clothes, sitting out, household storage — or recreation and grazing at arm's length? Ownership and history: was it always part of the property, or acquired later from an agricultural holding? And presentation: does it read as garden, or as a leftover paddock? Appeal decisions turn on small physical facts — a hedge, a gate, a change of levels, a post-and-rail fence breaking the visual and functional connection — and inspectors return, again and again, to separation, enclosure and domestic character.

title: The annex as loophole
paras: Now, the annex as loophole. 'Annex' has no protected planning meaning, and the word is now deployed as a strategy rather than a description. It carries emotional and political protection — care, family, support — while avoiding everything a new dwelling triggers: housing policy assessment, local plan targets, affordable housing thresholds, infrastructure contributions, the Community Infrastructure Levy. A well-worded annex application exists in a side lane of the system. Residential, but not a residence. Capable of housing people, but not counted as a planning unit. The question, always, is whether the accommodation remains ancillary to the host dwelling, or has become an independent planning unit. And that's judged by function, and by the relationship between the occupations — never by labels, kitchens or letterboxes.

The policy landscape invites the abuse. National policy doesn't define annexes, and most local plans are silent — or they offer one-line criteria, 'subordinate in scale', 'not capable of independent occupation', that are easy to write and impossible to apply. Every habitable room is capable of being slept in; once built, anything is capable of being sold. So decisions fall to officer discretion. Near-identical applications are treated differently within the same authority, agents learn where the lines are softer and build accordingly, and each permissive approval becomes the benchmark for the next. The term drifts from its meaning towards shorthand for 'just about permissible'.

The committee dynamics deserve naming, because the compassion card is real — and it works. The applicant attends with the elderly parent, or the child with additional needs. A member opens with 'this is just a family trying to look after each other'. The report's policy reasoning fades, and the proposal is approved. The circumstances are usually genuine. But planning is a land-use system, not a welfare assessment, and the case must stand on what is proposed and how it functions. Because the arrangement always changes. The parent dies, the adult child moves on, the annex stands empty — and then comes the application to remove the condition or formalise the dwelling, argued as a fallback from the authority's own approval. The sympathetic annex of 2025 becomes the bungalow of 2030. And across hundreds of cases, a shadow stock of homes accumulates — outside the housing supply counts, outside section 106, outside the Community Infrastructure Levy, outside any strategic oversight.

Control, where the principle is accepted, comes through conditions and obligations — and most fail through laziness, not illegality. A condition must pass the six P-P-G tests: necessary, relevant to planning, relevant to the development, enforceable, precise, and reasonable. And the recycled favourite — 'shall not be used other than incidentally to the dwelling', or 'occupied only by family members' — fails several at once. What is a family member? How is occupation monitored? What happens on inheritance? Where physical and functional separation is significant, and where occupancy limitation is what makes the scheme acceptable in principle, a section 106 does what conditions cannot: occupancy restrictions, non-severance clauses, reversion obligations, ties preventing a separate address or separate services — binding on the land, and on successors. But it must satisfy the regulation 122 tests — necessary, directly related, and fairly and reasonably related in scale. It cannot make fundamentally unacceptable development acceptable. And the P-P-G discourages reaching for an obligation where a condition would do.

title: When an annex becomes a house
paras: So when does an annex become a house? A material change of use from ancillary annex to independent dwelling is defined by function, not features. It doesn't happen with a kitchen installation, a letterbox, or rent changing hands. It happens when the accommodation is used independently of the host — sleeping, cooking and daily life conducted as a separate household. Moore against the Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government, reported at 2012, E-W-C-A Civ 1202, makes the point at Court of Appeal level. Sleeping accommodation alone does not create a separate planning unit — what matters is whether the character of the use has materially changed. Facilities matter as evidence: a building containing the means for daily living can no longer easily be called incidental. But the pattern of occupation decides. Are meals shared? Is there functional dependence on the main house? Would the building carry on as a home if the host were removed?

Structure and use are separate planning questions, and conflating them loses cases on both sides. A building lawfully erected under Class E is not thereby entitled to any use its owner chooses. The lawful structure does not legitimise the unlawful use, and the fallback argument — 'we built it under permitted development, you can't touch it' — fails where permitted development never applied, where the rights were withdrawn, or where the use cannot genuinely be reverted. Equally, enforcement must allege the right breach: the use, the structure, or both. A notice enforcing against a lawfully built structure, when the breach is the use, invites quashing. And the change is usually creep, not conversion. A shower, then a kitchenette. A sofa-bed. A lockable connecting door, separate heating, council tax registration. No single indicator is decisive alone; together, they point to independent occupation. Assess the matrix, not the item.

Two modern fronts widen the same battle. First, the garden office. Working from home is not the issue — the tipping point is regular client or customer visits, signage and advertising, deliveries and noise, dedicated business infrastructure, and scale. And the test is the character of the use, not harm or popularity: a use materially different from the residential one needs permission, even where nobody objects. Second, the short let. The courts recognised, before any legislation, that intensive holiday letting can be a material change of use — it comes down to intensity, turnover, and whether the property still behaves like a home or functions as serviced accommodation. And government has confirmed a dedicated short-term let use class, C5, with a national register, and permitted development rights removable by Article 4. The commencement position, and each authority's Article 4 stance, should be checked at the time of use.

Where an occupier seeks to regularise an independent use through a section 191 certificate, everything turns on evidence and time. The Levelling-up and Regeneration Act 2023, at section 115, abolished the four-year rule. Breaches now carry the ten-year period, with the old four-year rule preserved only where immunity had already accrued, or the breach occurred, before the 25th of April 2024. So certificates for recent dwellings must now prove a decade. Legacy applications still citing four years need checking against the transitional rules. And enforcement teams have a far longer window. The burden is the applicant's, on the balance of probabilities, through a continuous chain: utility accounts in the occupier's name, council tax for a second unit, tenancy agreements, the electoral roll, dated neighbour statements, photographs and floorplans. And Welwyn Hatfield against the Secretary of State for Communities and Local Government, reported at 2011, U-K-S-C 15, stands over it all. Deliberate concealment defeats the claim. The passage of time cannot immunise a dwelling hidden from the authority, and a certificate cannot reward deception.

title: Certificates: facts, not feelings
paras: Finally, certificates — facts, not feelings. A section 192 certificate asks: if I do this, will it be lawful? A section 191 certificate asks: I did this — is it now lawful, either because it never needed permission, or because the enforcement period has passed? In both, the key word is lawful. Not 'acceptable', not 'sensible', not 'supported by policy'. The authority does not investigate, does not weigh policy, does not balance harm. It tests the claim. If the evidence establishes it, the certificate must be issued. If not, it must be refused — however sympathetic the circumstances. The process is binary, and the onus is entirely on the applicant, on the balance of probabilities.

This is the most misused process in planning. Applicants believe, often with touching sincerity, that longevity plus a 'trusted builder' equals a rubber stamp. Certificates deal in facts, and facts need objective, consistent, compelling evidence — dated plans, utility records, tenancy agreements, council tax records, sworn declarations. The sort of evidence a tax inspector would take seriously, not a persuasive planning statement. The characteristic failures? Continuity gaps — the annex occupied for a year, then empty, then short-let. And absence of independence — all services, access and meals shared with the host, whoever slept where. The longer the period claimed, the higher the burden of proving it unbroken.

The abolition of the four-year rule reshaped this territory. The ten-year period now applies to operational development, new dwellings, changes of use and condition breaches alike, with the four-year rule preserved only where the breach, or the accrued immunity, predates the 25th of April 2024. Evidence standards for dwelling certificates have effectively doubled. 'No one complained' arguments fail without documentary proof. And Welwyn Hatfield, reported at 2011, U-K-S-C 15, polices the boundary: deliberate concealment — the dwelling disguised as a barn — invalidates the claim, regardless of time served. Honesty of use, and transparency to the authority, are part of what the certificate certifies.

A certificate is not a formality, and granting one has consequences beyond the site. Each annex certified as a dwelling enters the housing stock without ever passing through the plan-led process. The ancillary-use conditions fall away. Future control over sub-division, letting and extension weakens. And cumulatively, a village's certificates can add homes with no infrastructure reckoning. So the officer's discipline is this. Rigour at validation stage — require the full evidence set before the clock starts. Site visits that map the layout, the entrances, the services, the household behaviour. Alertness to concealment. Precisely drafted refusals. And a register of annex-based certificates, revisited over time — because certified sites drift too.
